name: Nebuchadnezzar
shortDescription: Nebuchadnezzar II was the king of Babylon who features prominently in the Book of Daniel, known for his military conquests and building projects but also his humbling experience and eventual recognition of God's sovereignty.
description: Nebuchadnezzar II, also known as Nebuchadrezzar II, reigned as the king of Babylon from 605 BC to 562 BC. He is a significant figure in the Bible, particularly in the Book of Daniel. Historically, he is known for his military campaigns that expanded the Babylonian Empire, including the siege and destruction of Jerusalem in 587/586 BC, leading to the Babylonian exile of the Jewish people. His reign also saw extensive building projects, most notably the reconstruction of Babylon, including the Ishtar Gate and the Hanging Gardens, considered one of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World. In the Book of Daniel, Nebuchadnezzar has several encounters with Daniel and his companions. He has a dream of a great statue made of different metals, which Daniel interprets as representing the succession of world empires that will eventually be replaced by God's eternal kingdom. Nebuchadnezzar also orders the construction of a golden image and commands everyone to worship it. Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego refuse and are thrown into a fiery furnace, but they are miraculously saved by God. Perhaps his most significant encounter is when Nebuchadnezzar boasts of his own power and glory, leading to a period of madness in which he lives like an animal for seven years. This experience humbles him, and at the end of his affliction, he acknowledges the sovereignty of God. While the biblical account presents a narrative of divine intervention and repentance, it is important to note that historical accounts primarily focus on his military and architectural achievements.

sources.title: Ancient Near Eastern Texts Relating to the Old Testament
sources.author: James B. Pritchard (Editor)

sources.title: The Cambridge Ancient History, Volume III, Part 2: The Assyrian and Babylonian Empires and Other States of the Near East, from the Eighth to the Sixth Centuries BC
sources.author: Various
